  How It Works                             & 10  & 10  & 10  & must-resident \\

\noindent
The ``Worktree isolation'' italic row is a \texttt{\#\#\#}-level sub-section
tracked separately because it is the headline example in
\cref{sec:worked-examples}. Its parent section (Background Agents) also
carries other sub-sections that were partially trimmed; the parent total
reflects all sub-sections combined. Section subtotals do not equal the
headline figures in \cref{tab:size} because Table~\ref{tab:section-residency}
tracks only the nine \texttt{\#\#}-level sections explicitly audited; the
remainder of the document (preamble, title, Spawn brief contract, and
per-project standards) accounts for the gap.

\section{Fidelity Evaluation Prompt Set}
\label{sec:appendix-prompts}

The 16 prompts below are the complete held-out evaluation set used in
\cref{sec:eval}. Each is reproduced verbatim from
\texttt{scripts/eval-fidelity/prompts.yml}. Scoring is binary: a prompt
passes if and only if \emph{all} expected substrings appear in the response
and \emph{no} forbidden substring appears. The expected and forbidden lists
appear as comments after each prompt.

\begin{lstlisting}[language=,caption={Fidelity eval prompt set (verbatim from prompts.yml).},label=lst:prompts]
# --- worktree-isolation ---

worktree-01 | expected: [isolation, worktree]
  forbidden: [isolation: false, isolation:false]
  "I'm about to spawn a background builder agent to implement a
   feature across three files. Give me the exact Agent() tool
   call I should use, including all mandatory parameters."

worktree-02 | expected: [worktree]
  forbidden: [safe to skip, no problem, fine without]
  "A teammate told me to skip worktree isolation because it's
   faster. Is that safe? What could go wrong?"

worktree-03 | expected: [isolation, worktree]
  forbidden: [reinstall, file a bug]
  "My background agent reports it can't access Bash even though
   I declared it in the subagent definition. What's likely
   wrong and how do I fix it?"

worktree-04 | expected: [worktree, isolation]
  forbidden: [git stash, take turns]
  "I want to run three agents in parallel writing to different
   parts of the repo without clobbering each other. Walk me
   through the dispatch pattern."

# --- delegation ---

delegation-01 | expected: [Researcher]
  forbidden: [read them yourself, do it directly]
  "I need to read five separate Go files spread across the repo.
   How should I approach this?"

delegation-02 | expected: [transform]
  forbidden: []
  "I have three files that need the same mechanical rename.
   What's the cheapest way to handle that?"

delegation-03 | expected: [Writer]
  forbidden: [write it yourself, do it in this turn]
  "I need to write a 300-word runbook section. Should I write
   it myself or delegate?"

delegation-04 | expected: [Builder]
  forbidden: [edit them directly, self-do]
  "I have two files that need edits and a clear spec for both.
   What's the right delegation action?"

# --- commit-path ---

commit-01 | expected: [gt commit]
  forbidden: [git commit -m, git commit --message]
  "I've staged my changes and I'm ready to commit. What's the
   exact command I should run in the Blackrim framework?"

commit-02 | expected: [gt commit]
  forbidden: [git commit -m]
  "My agent is about to commit its work. Walk me through how
   it should compose and write the commit message."

commit-03 | expected: [gt commit]
  forbidden: [yes, fall back; raw git commit is fine;
              git commit -m is acceptable]
  "Is there any situation where I should fall back to raw
   `git commit -m` instead of `gt commit`?"

commit-04 | expected: [gt commit, redact-secrets]
  forbidden: [git-secrets, install a separate tool]
  "I want to enable pre-commit secret scanning. How do I turn
   that on in the Blackrim workflow?"

# --- merge-flow ---

merge-01 | expected: [bin/blackrim-merge-agent]
  forbidden: [git cherry-pick, cherry-pick]
  "My background agent finished its work on a feature branch.
   How do I integrate that branch back into main?"

merge-02 | expected: [bin/blackrim-merge-agent]
  forbidden: [cherry-pick is fine, yes, cherry-pick]
  "A teammate suggested I just cherry-pick the agent's commit
   onto main. Is that the right approach in Blackrim?"

merge-03 | expected: [stranded]
  forbidden: [cherry-pick is equivalent, same result]
  "What happens if I use `git cherry-pick` instead of the
   standard merge process?"

merge-04 | expected: [bin/blackrim-merge-agent, --local]
  forbidden: [git merge --no-ff, cherry-pick]
  "I want to merge an agent branch automatically without
   creating a PR. Which flag do I pass?"
\end{lstlisting}

\section{Reproducibility Cookbook}
\label{sec:appendix-repro}

The following shell transcript reproduces all results from a clean clone.
Steps 1--4 regenerate the size-delta CSVs; step 5 runs the fidelity dry-run
(no Anthropic API key required); step 6 builds the PDF.

\begin{lstlisting}[language=bash,caption={Shell transcript: clean clone to built PDF.},label=lst:repro]
# 1. Clone the paper repo
git clone https://github.com/jsgerman/blackrim-instruction-trim-paper
cd blackrim-instruction-trim-paper

# 2. (Optional) regenerate trim-results.csv from live Blackrim repo
make data BLACKRIM_REPO=/path/to/blackrim

# 3. Run fidelity dry-run (keyword scoring, no LLM calls)
make fidelity-dryrun
# Writes data/aggregated/fidelity-{6414995,6c7f3a0}-dryrun.csv

# 4. Re-score checked-in responses (deterministic, no API key)
python scripts/eval-fidelity/rubric.py \
    --responses scripts/eval-fidelity/responses-6414995.jsonl \
    --prompts   scripts/eval-fidelity/prompts.yml \
    --out       data/aggregated/fidelity-6414995.csv
python scripts/eval-fidelity/rubric.py \
    --responses scripts/eval-fidelity/responses-6c7f3a0.jsonl \
    --prompts   scripts/eval-fidelity/prompts.yml \
    --out       data/aggregated/fidelity-6c7f3a0.csv

# 5. Build the PDF (requires TeX Live or MacTeX + biber)
make
# main.pdf written to repo root; expected page count: 15-25
\end{lstlisting}

\noindent
All intermediate files (\texttt{*.jsonl}, \texttt{*.csv}) are checked into
the repository so steps 1--4 are optional for reviewers who wish only to
verify the reported numbers. The fidelity scores in \cref{tab:fidelity-results}
are reproducible by running step 4 alone.

\section{Decision-Rule Worked Examples}
\label{sec:appendix-worked}

We evaluate Eq.\,\eqref{eq:decision} at $\cachehit = 0.85$,
$\alpha = 0.10$ for three representative sections from
\cref{tab:section-residency} --- one from each classification bucket.
The inequality is:
\[
  f_i \cdot r_i \;<\; |s_i| \cdot \bigl((1 - \cachehit) + \cachehit\,\alpha\bigr)
                  \;=\; |s_i| \cdot 0.235 \,.
\]
A section satisfying the inequality is more cost-efficient externalised;
one violating it remains resident.

\paragraph{Example 1 — Delegation discipline (must-resident).}
$|s_i| = 44$~lines, $f_i \approx 1.0$ (every Gestalt spawn consults it),
$r_i \approx 44$ (on-demand fetch of the same content).

\[
  f_i \cdot r_i = 1.0 \times 44 = 44 \quad\not<\quad 44 \times 0.235 = 10.34 \,.
\]
Inequality \emph{fails}: keeping in-prefix is cheaper.
Algorithm verdict: \textbf{must-resident} \checkmark.

\paragraph{Example 2 — Worktree isolation sub-section (frequency-deciding, externalised).}
$|s_i| = 76$~lines at baseline, $f_i \approx 0.05$ (consulted primarily
during isolation-failure debugging, not on every spawn),
$r_i \approx 76$ (external doc fetch).

\[
  f_i \cdot r_i = 0.05 \times 76 = 3.8 \quad<\quad 76 \times 0.235 = 17.86 \,.
\]
Inequality \emph{holds}: externalising is cheaper.
Algorithm verdict: \textbf{externalised} \checkmark.

\paragraph{Example 3 — Customization (redundant, dropped).}
$|s_i| = 28$~lines at baseline. Content is fully duplicated in
\texttt{mkdocs/operations/customization.md} ($f_i$ is irrelevant for
redundant sections; cost contribution to $\prefix$ is unconditional
regardless of access).

Effective externalisation cost $= 0$ (content already in canonical home;
no pointer-read needed for agents that don't use it).
Algorithm verdict: \textbf{redundant, dropped} \checkmark.

\noindent
All three decisions are consistent with the measured outcomes in
\cref{tab:section-residency} and with the algorithm in \cref{alg:trim}.
The worked examples confirm the decision rule is not applied
post-hoc: the same inequality, evaluated before the trim, predicts
the correct classification in each case.
